title: Discipleship 11: The Role of Parents in Discipleship, Part 3 — Biblical How-Tos
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 11 - The Role of Parents in Discipleship - Part 3 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Gospel-Centered New Creature as Foundation for Parenting
subpoints: Christ's redemptive work recreates us as new creatures (2 Corinthians 5:17); we now sit in heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6)
Because of Christ: wife submits, husband loves, child obeys parents, parents raise children in the Lord
The focus of this lesson: biblical how-tos of child-rearing from Ephesians 6 and Colossians 3
Quick harmony of both passages reveals seven shared truths about parenting and being parented

outline.title: Seven Truths from Ephesians 6 and Colossians 3
subpoints: 1. The glory of God is the goal of both parenting and being parented (both passages say 'in the Lord' / 'as is well pleasing to the Lord')
2. Gospel obedience has no other limitation than the Word of God ('in all things')
3. Parenting methods and styles can easily discourage children — Paul warns against this in both texts
4. Fathers naturally tend to exasperate their children (both passages address the father specifically)
5. The basis for obedience is the moral law — Paul quotes the Fifth Commandment to children
6. Fathers must play an active and central role in upbringing: 'bring them up' (Ephesians 6:4) is the father's active duty
7. There are benefits for obeying God in this area — the promise attached to the Fifth Commandment

outline.title: Children Are Members of the Visible Church
subpoints: Paul addresses children in Ephesians (written 'to the saints in Ephesus') — therefore children are part of the church
A baptized child of a member of the church is also a member of the church
The status change when a child professes faith is from covenant member to communing member — not from non-member to member
Practical implications: raise children as Christians, not as little pagans; press the gospel as to one who belongs to God
We already practice this without realizing it — we teach children to pray, to obey parents 'in the Lord,' to call God 'Father'

outline.title: Do Not Provoke or Exasperate (Two Negative Commands)
subpoints: Ephesians 6: 'do not provoke to wrath' — the Greek means 'do not make angry'; if our parenting style causes anger, the guilt is partly ours
Colossians 3: 'do not embitter' — 'cause them to be bitter'; the greatest sources of bitterness: hypocrisy, inconsistency, harsh words, broken promises, excessive criticism, conditional love
Moving targets for obedience exasperate children: punished harshly today, ignored for the same thing tomorrow
These negatives discourage children from obeying us and by implication from obeying the Lord who called them to obey parents

outline.title: Positive Commands: Training and Admonition in the Lord (Deuteronomy 6)
subpoints: Ephesians 6:4: 'bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord' — training = what is done to the child (rules, structure, spanking, modeling); admonition = what is said to the child
Deuteronomy 6:7: teach them diligently — formal settings (family devotions, deliberate teaching times)
Deuteronomy 6:7-9: informal instruction — the Word of God governs every interaction: sitting, walking, lying down, rising up; bound on hands, forehead, doorposts
Parents are responsible for all influences on their children; every influence is a teacher
Teach by example (Philippians 4:9); beware turning every situation into a sermon
Fathers are responsible for knowing what their children's teachers are teaching them in every setting (school, church, friends)
1 Corinthians 15:33: evil company corrupts good habits — guard against destructive influences

summary:
This session addresses the positive biblical how-tos of raising children, working through a harmony of Ephesians 6:1-4 and Colossians 3:20-21 and then digging into the practical instruction of Deuteronomy 6. Pastor Lyro begins by reminding the congregation that all Christian obedience — including parenting — flows from the new creature reality brought about by Christ's redemptive work. Because of Christ, children obey parents, and parents raise their children in the Lord.

A quick harmony of Ephesians 6 and Colossians 3 yields seven shared observations: the glory of God is the goal of both parenting and being parented; the only limitation on gospel obedience is the Word of God; certain parenting styles naturally discourage children; fathers specifically tend to exasperate their children; obedience is grounded in the moral law (the Fifth Commandment); fathers must take an active and central role; and God attaches a blessing to this obedience. An important application flows from the fact that Paul addresses children as part of the church at Ephesus: baptized children are members of the church, covenant members who are to be raised as Christians — not as pagans who must become Christians from scratch.

Two negative commands are then unpacked: do not provoke your children to wrath (Ephesians 6:4, meaning do not make them angry through an unreasonable parenting style), and do not embitter your children (Colossians 3:21, meaning do not make them bitter). The primary sources of bitterness are identified as hypocrisy, inconsistency, harsh words, broken promises, excessive criticism, and conditional love. These negatives discourage children not only from obeying their parents but from obeying the Lord who commands that obedience.

The session then turns to the positive commands. Training involves what is done to and for children (structure, discipline, modeling); admonition involves what is said to children. Deuteronomy 6 provides the framework: the Word of God must dominate every aspect of family life — formal devotions, informal conversation at every moment of the day, and the modeling of parents' own lives. Fathers are ultimately responsible for knowing what all the teachers in their children's lives are teaching them, since every influence is a teacher.
transcript:
## Biblical How-Tos of Child-Rearing

We continue our series on discipleship, looking at the parent-child relationship. We have looked at the covenantal basis for raising children, and now we turn to the practical how-tos. Our two key texts are Ephesians 6:1–4 and Colossians 3:20–21, which Paul addresses to the church — including the children, who are members of the body of Christ.

## A Quick Harmony of the Two Passages

Before we address the how-tos, several observations from putting Ephesians 6 and Colossians 3 together:

- **The glory of God is the goal** of both parenting and being parented. Both passages frame obedience as being "in the Lord" or "well pleasing to the Lord."
- **Godly obedience has no other limitation than the word of God.** In Colossians 3:20, "all things" defines the scope — meaning every area of life, limited only by what Scripture says.
- **Parenting methods can easily discourage children.** Both passages warn against provoking and discouraging children.
- **Fathers are specifically addressed.** In both passages Paul could have said "parents," but he shifts to "fathers" — because fathers are more naturally prone to exasperate their children.
- **The basis for obedience is the moral law.** Paul quotes the fifth commandment to the children in both letters. He is not afraid to say: you must do this.
- **There are real blessings in obedience.** Paul calls the command to honor parents "the first commandment with a promise."

## Children Are Members of the Church

The children are addressed directly in both Ephesians and Colossians as part of the congregation. Paul is writing to the saints in Ephesus and includes children as part of those saints. A baptized child of a church member is a member of the church. Their status as covenant members changes when they make a public profession of faith — not from non-member to member, but from covenant member to communicant member.

This means we raise our children as Christians, not as pagans. We press the gospel upon them not as if they have no relationship with God, but as those who belong to him. We teach them to pray, to call God their Father, to know Jesus as Lord — because that is what they are, covenantally. Their regeneration may not have happened yet, but their covenant standing is real.

## Two Negatives: What Not to Do

**Do not provoke your children to anger** (Ephesians 6:4). The word means *make angry*. Your children may become angry at you — and if they have no legitimate reason, that is on them, not you. But if your harsh, graceless, unreasonable way of parenting is the cause of their anger, the guilt belongs partly to you.

**Do not embitter your children** (Colossians 3:21). The word means *cause to become bitter*. Common sources of bitterness in children: hypocrisy in their parents, self-righteousness, conditional love — love withheld when they fail to perform as expected. One New Testament scholar writes: obedience must never be made the condition of parental love. Love conditioned on performance is not love; it is self-interest dressed up as love.

Another source of bitterness is inconsistency — a moving target for obedience. Today a child is disciplined severely for one thing; the next day the same thing passes without notice. That inconsistency exasperates and discourages.

Both these negatives ultimately discourage children not just from obeying us, but from obeying the Lord, since the Lord is the one who called them to obey their parents.

## The Positive Call: Training and Admonition

> *"Bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord."* (Ephesians 6:4)

**Training** refers primarily to what is *done* to the child: structure, rules, modeling, and discipline (including spanking — Proverbs is explicit that the rod is a tool of love, not hatred).

**Admonition** refers primarily to what is *said* to the child: verbal instruction, correction, encouragement.

Both are to be *of the Lord* — meaning he sets the standard, he defines the content, and he is the goal.

## The Word of God, Formal and Informal

Deuteronomy 6:7–9 gives us the framework for how to keep the word of God before our children:

> *"You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, when you walk by the way, when you lie down, and when you rise up. You shall bind them as a sign on your hand, and they shall be as frontlets between your eyes. You shall write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates."*

**Formally**: Have regular family devotions. Keep them brief, especially with young children. Sing a hymn together. Read a short section of Scripture. Ask each family member a simple question. Pray together — not only for your children, but *with* them. Use the catechism as a teaching tool. The dinner table is one of the most sacred spaces in the home for this kind of formation.

**Informally**: As you go through life, you are always teaching. Every interaction governed by the word of God is instruction. Jesus was a peripatetic teacher — he taught as he and his disciples did life together. *"Look at the flowers of the field..."* — Scripture unfolding right there in the middle of the road. When your teenager finally wants to talk at 11:30 at night, cancel whatever else you were planning and be present. Those are the teaching moments you cannot schedule.

Answer your children's questions with God's truth. Teach them by example — Paul says: *"The things which you learned and received and heard and saw in me, these do, and the God of peace will be with you."* (Philippians 4:9) That is a bold statement. We need to be able to say to our children: follow what you see in me.

## The Goal: Not Well-Behaved Children but Believing Children

Strict discipline is not sufficient. It can channel sin in socially acceptable ways; it cannot change a heart. Our ultimate goal is not children who say "please" and "thank you" and excel at school. Our goal is children who know and trust the Lord Jesus Christ.

You are responsible before God to train your children. Not the government. Not the Christian school. Not the church. You — and especially you, fathers. These institutions can assist; the responsibility is yours. Every influence is a teacher, and you are responsible to know what teachers are teaching your children.

Above all: enjoy your children. They are a heritage from the Lord, a reward, arrows in the hand of a warrior.

> *"Happy is the man who has his quiver full of them."* (Psalm 127:5)

They are not ready-made blessings — they require work to become the blessings God intends them to be. But they are his gift, given to you to steward for his glory. Disciple them faithfully, discipline them lovingly, delight in them genuinely.
